evidence: crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:39-59 `connect_postgres_store` performs connection plus two migration flows (`temper_store_postgres::migration::run_migrations` and `PostgresRecordStore::migrate`), so initialization side effects are embedded in what appears to be a pure connector boundary.
crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:212-368 and 297-369 duplicate near-identical registry restoration flows for Postgres and Turso, creating backend-branch coupling in the CLI layer.

crates/temper-server/src/events.rs:46 and crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch_blocking.rs:24-50 both reach directly into `ServerState` as a shared hub (broadcast channel subscription + internal WASM dispatch forwarding), increasing blast radius for state-surface changes.

crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs:48-447 combines three distinct generation pipelines (insights, unmet intents, feature requests), threshold policy, and error taxonomy in one module, mixing responsibilities that change for different reasons.

crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:30-50 and 92-109 dynamically merge persisted JSON blobs into top-level response objects, making endpoint contract shape depend on storage payload internals rather than explicit DTO ownership.

crates/temper-cli/src/init/mod.rs:8-41 hardcodes scaffold templates and directory layout directly in command code, coupling CLI behavior to one evolving project shape without an explicit template boundary.

Tenant dispatch and actor-spawn APIs are layered as pass-through wrappers (`dispatch_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_ext` -> `dispatch_tenant_action_core`; `get_or_spawn_actor` -> `get_or_spawn_tenant_actor` -> `get_or_spawn_tenant_actor_with_fields`) with behavior split mostly by optional defaults rather than distinct abstractions.

OData helpers still carry dual-path compatibility fallbacks (`resolve_entity_type`, `tenant_csdl_xml`, `tenant_entity_sets`) that bridge new registry and legacy maps, keeping the boundary leaky instead of committing to one model.
A crate-local utility module exists solely to re-export naming helpers (`crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs`), adding an extra alias layer without policy or translation.

`temper-cli` serve path directly imports and compiles against Postgres (`sqlx`), Turso/libsql, Redis, ClickHouse observe store, and platform/server orchestration in one module (`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs`), creating high dependency fan-in for a single command surface.
Storage-specific behavior is selected at runtime rather than isolated behind feature-gated backends, so dependency weight and compile coupling remain high even when only one backend is used.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Runtime backend switching in one module keeps all backend crates in the command’s direct dependency surface.

evidence: IOA-to-runtime translation logic is duplicated across `temper-jit` (`TransitionTable::from_automaton`) and `temper-verify` model builder (`translate_guards`/`translate_effects` + name-heuristic fallbacks), creating seam drift risk for guard/effect semantics.

Authz boundary repeatedly converts deterministic `BTreeMap` inputs into `HashMap` at multiple call sites (`entity_ops`, `wasm_authz_gate`) because the engine API requires `HashMap`, adding repeated boundary churn instead of centralizing translation once.

Persistence seam for evolution records is implemented as separate ad-hoc helper flows (`platform/evolution/feedback.rs` and `server/observe/evolution/operations.rs`) with different async patterns and error handling behavior.
`crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:27` uses `#[allow(deprecated)]` on a public re-export, keeping deprecated API visible without migration pressure.
`crates/temper-evolution/src/records.rs:347` truncates UUID v7 to 4 chars for record IDs, creating a high collision surface as record volume grows.
`crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:27-31,55` mixes `anyhow::Result` with `ToolResult::Error(String)`, forcing two independent failure channels.

`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs` wires multiple mutating `/api/*` routes directly to `crate::observe::*` handlers (`specs`, `wasm`, `evolution`), so file placement does not match the API boundary described in module docs.

`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution.rs` exports both read endpoints (`list_evolution_records`, `get_evolution_record`) and write endpoints (`handle_decide`, `handle_unmet_intent`), indicating mixed command/query ownership in one package area.

`holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles["crates/temper-server/src/observe/"]` reports 10 production files in one folder with mixed concerns (agents/entities/metrics/specs/verification/wasm/evolution), reducing navigability of ownership boundaries.

`crates/temper-server/src/api.rs` applies Cedar checks for some sibling endpoints (`handle_put_policies`, `handle_add_policy_rule`, `handle_approve_decision`, `handle_deny_decision`) but leaves other sensitive endpoints ungated (`handle_put_secret`, `handle_delete_secret`, `handle_list_secrets`, `handle_get_policies`, `handle_list_decisions`, `handle_list_all_decisions`, `handle_repl`, `handle_audit`).

`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs` defaults missing/unknown principal headers to `PrincipalKind::System`, and `crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs` makes `authorize_or_bypass` always allow `System`, creating an implicit bypass path when callers do not enforce identity.

`crates/temper-server/src/observe/entities.rs`, `crates/temper-server/src/observe/agents.rs`, and `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs` expose operational/tenant activity and include mutating behavior (`handle_sentinel_check`, `handle_update_feature_request`) without authorization checks.

`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs` and several API handlers use implicit tenant fallback (`first registered` or `default`) when `x-tenant-id` is missing, producing inconsistent trust boundaries across handlers.

findings.summary: Storage-specific orchestration is embedded in entrypoint modules instead of behind a backend-neutral boundary.
evidence: crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:39-59 runs connection + migrations + evolution table migration in one function, binding startup behavior to Postgres implementation details.
crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:212-368 and 297-369 repeat backend-specific load/rehydration code paths instead of one shared restore workflow.

crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:24-27 and 89-90 call Turso-specific APIs directly from handlers (`list_evolution_records`, `list_ranked_insights`), so HTTP layer is coupled to one concrete persistence API.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a small persistence facade trait for evolution/spec read-write and migration hooks; make CLI serve startup and observe handlers depend on that facade so backend branching and migration sequencing live in one backend adapter layer.

findings.summary: Evolution insight policy and output contract ownership are split across ad hoc modules.
evidence: crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs:48-419 includes multiple domain responsibilities (aggregation, unmet-intent resolution logic, feature-request generation, threshold constants, error taxonomy) in a single module.

crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:43-50 and 100-107 flatten arbitrary JSON `data` into endpoint payloads, so externally visible shape is implicitly driven by whichever generator wrote the blob.
The combined pattern makes domain policy edits and API response edits cross-cut each other, increasing ripple and reducing predictable ownership.
findings.suggestion: Split evolution into explicit submodules (`insight_rules`, `unmet_intent_rules`, `feature_request_rules`) and define typed response DTOs for record-list handlers instead of JSON field merging.

findings.summary: `ServerState` is used as a broad integration hub across unrelated concerns, increasing coupling.
evidence: crates/temper-server/src/events.rs:43-58 directly subscribes to `state.event_tx`, binding SSE transport to internal state field layout.
crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch_blocking.rs:24-50 extends `impl ServerState` with inline WASM orchestration and forwards an 8-field request bag to internal dispatch.
Both modules attach dissimilar concerns (SSE transport and integration dispatch choreography) to the same shared state object, signaling god-object growth at subsystem boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Extract focused service interfaces (for example `EventStreamService` and `IntegrationDispatchService`) owned by `ServerState` but consumed by handlers/modules via narrow traits, so boundary changes don’t require touching the global state surface.

findings.summary: Tenant/registry migration left a low-leverage wrapper and fallback stack that obscures true boundaries.
evidence: `dispatch_action` only forwards to tenant-aware variants with default tenant before reaching core logic, while actor spawn has the same wrapper chain pattern.
OData helper layer still implements registry-first then legacy fallback reads for entity resolution and metadata, signaling unresolved boundary ownership between old and new models.
`crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs` is a pure re-export shim with no added behavior.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrapper chains into one public tenant-aware API plus a narrow compatibility adapter (or remove legacy adapter entirely), and delete pure re-export shims by importing `temper_spec::naming` directly at call sites.

findings.summary: Guard/effect semantics are reimplemented in multiple modules, creating integration-seam drift risk.
evidence: `temper-jit` builds transition guards/effects from IOA action definitions, while `temper-verify` independently maps IOA guards/effects into model guards/effects with separate fallback heuristics.
`sim_handler` additionally maps parsed assertion expressions into runtime invariants in another local conversion path.
findings.suggestion: Extract one shared IOA semantic translation crate/module (guards, effects, invariant assertion mapping) used by JIT table building, verify model building, and sim handler conversion, then keep only representation-specific final mapping locally.

findings.summary: Authz seam forces repeated map-type conversion at call sites, causing boundary glue duplication.
evidence: Both entity dispatch authz and WASM authz gate build `BTreeMap<String, Value>` then convert to `HashMap` right before calling `authorize_or_bypass`.
The conversion logic is duplicated in multiple call paths solely to satisfy engine API type expectations.
findings.suggestion: Change `AuthzEngine` input to `impl IntoIterator<Item = (String, Value)>` or a deterministic map type accepted by all callers, and centralize canonicalization inside the engine.

findings.summary: Simulation handler duplicates transition eligibility logic instead of reusing table evaluation entrypoints.
evidence: `valid_actions` in `sim_handler` manually iterates `table.rules`, checks `from_states`, and invokes guard checks directly.
Action execution path uses `process_action`/`evaluate_ctx`, so eligibility and execution selection logic are maintained via different code paths.
findings.suggestion: Add a single `TransitionTable::valid_actions_ctx(...)` (or equivalent helper) and use it from both simulation inspection (`valid_actions`) and execution paths to eliminate duplicated branching rules.

findings.summary: `temper serve` module has high direct backend dependency fan-in without compile-time isolation.
evidence: `serve/mod.rs` directly references Postgres/sqlx, Turso/libsql, Redis store, observe/ClickHouse, and server/platform state wiring in one command module.
Backend selection is runtime-switched, so the command module remains coupled to all backends and their transitive crates.
findings.suggestion: Introduce feature-gated backend adapters (or per-backend submodules) so each build includes only selected backend dependency trees and the main command module depends on a narrower trait surface.

findings.summary: Deprecated API is kept alive via crate-root allow, masking incomplete migration.
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:27-28` has `#[allow(deprecated)] pub use store::RecordStore;`.
No local deprecation-removal guard or migration boundary exists in this crate root; the deprecated surface is still exported as first-class API.
findings.suggestion: Remove the deprecated root re-export and migrate call sites to the replacement store API; if temporary compatibility is required, gate it behind a feature flag with a documented removal version/date.

findings.summary: Record ID generation truncates UUID entropy to 16 bits, making collisions plausible.
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/records.rs:347` computes `seq` as `&uuid::Uuid::now_v7().to_string()[..4]`.
This yields only 4 hex chars (65,536 possibilities) per prefix/year namespace, which is too small for durable immutable record IDs.
findings.suggestion: Use the full UUID (or at least a much longer suffix) in `RecordHeader::new`, and add a regression test that generates many IDs and asserts uniqueness under load-like counts.

findings.summary: Tool execution API uses two error channels, increasing branch complexity and inconsistency.
evidence: `crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:27-32` defines `ToolResult::{Success, Error}`.
`crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:55` also returns `anyhow::Result<ToolResult>`, so failures can be `Err(_)` or `Ok(ToolResult::Error(_))`.
`crates/temper-mcp/src/error.rs` shows a typed single-channel approach (`McpResult<T>`) already used elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one failure channel: return `Result<ToolOutput, ToolError>` (typed enum) and remove `ToolResult::Error`, so callers handle errors in one predictable path.

findings.summary: Error enums rely on raw String payloads, reducing precision and encouraging message-parsing logic.
evidence: `crates/temper-authz/src/error.rs` variants (`Denied`, `PolicyParse`, `Engine`, etc.) all carry `String` payloads.
`crates/temper-observe/src/error.rs` similarly uses string payloads for `InvalidQuery`, `UnknownColumn`, `ProviderError`, `ConnectionError`.
Only serialization keeps typed source (`SerializationError(#[from] serde_json::Error)`), showing inconsistency inside the same error style.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed payloads/source errors for parse/provider/transport categories (or structured context fields) and reserve free-form strings for final display formatting.

findings.summary: Single-line facade modules add migration-era indirection without local behavior.
evidence: `crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs:1-3` is only a doc comment plus `pub use temper_sandbox::repl::{...}`.
`crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs:1-3` is only a doc comment plus `pub(crate) use temper_spec::naming::{...}`.
These shims add extra navigation and ownership ambiguity while providing no policy, validation, or adaptation.
findings.suggestion: Either inline direct imports at call sites or expand these modules to own real boundary behavior (validation/policy/translation); avoid passive re-export-only files.

findings.summary: /api mutation routes are implemented in observe modules, inverting folder ownership boundaries
evidence: `api.rs` registers POST/DELETE mutation routes to `crate::observe::specs::*`, `crate::observe::wasm::*`, and `crate::observe::evolution::*`.
`observe/evolution.rs` exports mutating handlers (`handle_decide`, `handle_unmet_intent`) together with read handlers, so router-level command/query separation is not reflected in package layout.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `crates/temper-server/src/api/` submodules (`specs.rs`, `wasm.rs`, `evolution.rs`) as the command-side home; 2) Move mutating handlers from `observe/*` into those `api/*` modules in this order: specs/wasm first, then evolution write handlers; 3) Keep `observe/*` with GET/read handlers only and update imports in `build_api_router`; 4) Run import/update checks and validation with `rg -n "crate::observe::(specs|wasm|evolution)::" crates/temper-server/src -g '*.rs'`, `cargo check -p temper-server`, and `cargo test -p temper-server`.

findings.summary: observe package is a mixed-capability bucket instead of capability-scoped subfolders
evidence: The same `observe/` folder contains unrelated capability files (`agents`, `entities`, `metrics`, `verification`, `wasm`, `specs`, `evolution`) with both read and write behavior exposed via re-exports.
Directory profile data shows 10 production files in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/`, which is already a dense flat area with mixed concerns and no clear sub-boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Introduce capability subfolders under `observe/` such as `observe/entities/`, `observe/agents/`, `observe/evolution/read/`; 2) Move files by capability starting with leaf modules (`agents.rs`, `entities.rs`) then split `evolution.rs` into read-only facade + internal modules; 3) Update `observe/mod.rs` exports to keep a narrow, predictable public surface; 4) Validate import rewrites and routing with `rg -n "mod (agents|entities|evolution)|use crate::observe::" crates/temper-server/src/observe -g '*.rs'`, then `cargo check -p temper-server` and `cargo test -p temper-server observe::mod_test`.

findings.summary: authz_helpers module hides domain orchestration behind helper naming
evidence: `record_authz_denial` performs three domain writes/orchestrations: `persist_pending_decision`, `dispatch_tenant_action` (GovernanceDecision), and `persist_trajectory_entry`.
`api.rs` authorization flow calls this function directly, so decision/audit workflow ownership is centralized in a helper file rather than an explicit service boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Split `authz_helpers.rs` into `authz/headers.rs` (header/context parsing) and `authz/denial_service.rs` (decision/audit workflow); 2) Move write-side orchestration (`record_authz_denial`) into `denial_service` first, then update `api.rs` call sites to service-oriented naming; 3) Keep helper module pure/side-effect free; 4) Run import-update and validation commands: `rg -n "record_authz_denial|security_context_from_headers" crates/temper-server/src -g '*.rs'`, `cargo check -p temper-server`, `cargo test -p temper-server`.

findings.summary: temper-observe crate root exposes two different subsystem roles through one flat API
evidence: `lib.rs` re-exports query-facing store abstractions (`ObservabilityStore`, `ClickHouseStore`) and write/emission functions (`emit_span`, `emit_metrics`, `from_transition`) at the same top level.
`clickhouse.rs` is explicitly query-only, but crate root does not partition query vs emission entry points, so ownership boundaries are unclear for consumers.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create top-level namespaces `query` and `emit` (for example `src/query/{store.rs,clickhouse.rs,memory.rs}` and `src/emit/{otel.rs,wide_event.rs,trajectory.rs}`); 2) Move provider/query files first, then emission files; 3) Keep `lib.rs` as a thin facade with grouped re-exports (`pub mod query`, `pub mod emit`) instead of mixed flat exports; 4) Validate import updates with `rg -n "temper_observe::(ClickHouseStore|emit_span|ObservabilityStore|from_transition)" -g '*.rs'` and run `cargo check -p temper-observe -p temper-server` plus `cargo test -p temper-observe`.

findings.summary: Evolution read APIs violate the documented Postgres/Turso compatibility contract
evidence: README.md claims durable state/event sourcing on "Postgres or Turso/libSQL" and marks Evolution Engine as implemented.
crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs queries `state.persistent_store()` (Turso) and falls back to empty records/insights when absent.
crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs explicitly supports Postgres persistence and migration paths, indicating backend duality exists elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Define an evolution read-store contract that is backend-agnostic (trait or enum-backed adapter), then route `records_list` through it. Ensure fallback is an explicit error/reporting mode rather than silent empty data so runtime behavior matches README guarantees.

findings.summary: Evolution domain logic is split across root and observe/evolution boundaries
evidence: crates/temper-server/src/insight_generator.rs implements unmet-intent and feature-request generation (core evolution behavior).
crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs exposes evolution records/insights APIs, creating a separate evolution locus.
README.md describes Evolution Engine as a single coherent capability, but runtime ownership is split across unrelated package levels.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate evolution pipeline code under one subsystem boundary (for example `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/`), with clear internal modules for generation, storage access, and HTTP projection; keep root-level exports minimal and domain-oriented.

findings.summary: Server root package is overloaded and contains evolution-specific stragglers
evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `crates/temper-server/src/` has 22 files, indicating flat mixed concerns beyond the >10 threshold.
`insight_generator.rs` has strong import-affinity to evolution concerns (insight/unmet-intent/feature-request records) but is not colocated with `observe/evolution/*` endpoints.
This layout weakens discoverability for governance/evolution workflows described in README.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) create/confirm target folder `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/` as the canonical evolution package; 2) move `insight_generator.rs` to `observe/evolution/insight_generator.rs`; 3) update module wiring/re-exports (`observe/evolution.rs` and any `mod`/`use` sites); 4) run import/update checks with `rg -n "insight_generator" crates/temper-server/src`, then `cargo fmt` and `cargo test -p temper-server`; 5) if stable, continue by moving adjacent evolution-only root files in small batches using the same validate loop.

findings.summary: Postgres/Turso mirror implementations create architecture drift risk
evidence: crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs duplicates orchestration in `load_registry_from_postgres` vs `load_registry_from_turso` and `upsert_loaded_specs_to_postgres` vs `upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso`.
The duplication encodes backend policy in parallel paths; README’s backend-agnostic messaging increases the cost of keeping both paths behaviorally aligned.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared persistence adapter interface for specs/constraints load+upsert and keep backend-specific SQL/client details behind adapter impls; enforce parity with backend-parameterized tests to prevent future contract divergence.

findings.summary: Sensitive `/api` routes are inconsistently Cedar-gated, leaving sibling management endpoints open.
evidence: `handle_put_policies`, `handle_add_policy_rule`, and decision approve/deny call authorization checks, but secret CRUD/list and policy read/list decisions endpoints do not perform equivalent checks.
Cross-tenant decision listing/stream endpoints (`/api/decisions`, `/api/decisions/stream`) lack the same gate applied to tenant decision mutation.
README claims Cedar authorization mediates every action, but route-level coverage is selective.
findings.suggestion: Add a shared authorization middleware/helper for all management endpoints (including read/list/stream and secret routes), with explicit action/resource mappings, and fail closed when auth headers are absent.

findings.summary: Missing principal headers default to `System`, which is unconditionally allowed by authz bypass logic.
evidence: `SecurityContext::from_headers` initializes `kind` as `PrincipalKind::System` when headers are absent/unknown.
`AuthzEngine::authorize_or_bypass` immediately returns `Allow` for `System` principals.

API handlers build security context directly from request headers (`security_context_from_headers`) and then rely on `authorize_with_context`, so omitted identity headers can inherit bypass semantics.
findings.suggestion: Change default principal to non-privileged (for example `Agent`/`Customer` anonymous), reserve `System` for internal trusted call paths only, and reject external requests that do not present required identity headers.

findings.summary: Observe endpoints expose broad tenant data and allow mutations without a consistent auth policy.
evidence: `list_entities` returns actor registry contents without principal/tenant authorization checks.
`list_agents` and `get_agent_history` expose trajectory-derived agent data with optional tenant filtering but no enforced permission check.
`handle_sentinel_check` (POST) and `handle_update_feature_request` (PATCH) mutate evolution records/state without Cedar authorization.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce an explicit observe permission model (read_observe, manage_evolution, etc.), apply it uniformly at router boundaries, and require tenant-scoped authorization before returning or mutating observe data.

findings.summary: Handlers silently infer tenant when headers are missing, causing inconsistent and risky tenant scoping.
evidence: `extract_tenant` falls back to first registered tenant, then `TenantId::default()` if header is absent.
`handle_authorize`, `handle_audit`, and `handle_repl` default tenant to `default` when `x-tenant-id` is missing.
Some handlers use inferred tenant while others use path tenant or no tenant filter, producing inconsistent authorization scope behavior.
findings.suggestion: Make tenant identity explicit and required on external requests, reject missing/invalid tenant headers, and centralize tenant resolution so all route families share the same fail-closed behavior.
